text: The surgeon begins the myomectomy by dissecting the pedicle of a large calcified subserous fibroid, revealing its intramural component. The procedure continues with the dissection and removal of additional intramural and subserous fibroids as identified on the MRI.

The surgeon identifies severe endometriosis along the tubal margins and ovarian surfaces, performing ovarial lysis by releasing endometriotic adhesions. Bilateral endometriosis and a small component of deep endometriosis are visible, with the pouch of Douglas now completely released from prior obliteration.

The surgeon performs gentle vasopressin infiltration with approximately seven units in 100 ml of normal saline. Following this, the dissection continues to release the posterior submucous, subserous, and intramural fibroids. Three fibroids are visibly released from the posterior aspect of the uterus.
The surgeon uses gentle bipolar current to manage bleeders and maintain the plane of dissection while separating fibroids from the myoma bed, ensuring minimal damage to the uterine musculature.

The surgeon sutures the opened myoma beds on both the anterior and posterior aspects of the uterus using a quill suture, an interlocking technique applied in multiple layers. The cavity is carefully obliterated to prevent hematoma formation, ensuring the integrity of the myoma tissue for future conception.
The surgeon places the fibroids inside a specialized containment bag to perform morcellation, preventing spillage of fibroid tissue into the peritoneal cavity during the procedure.

The surgeon introduces the morcellator into the more safe bag containing the fibroid specimens through one of its lateral ends. The fibroids are gently morcellated within the bag to prevent spillage into the peritoneal cavity. The specimens are then sent for histopathological examination. The surgical team adjusts the patient's position, reducing head low, to prevent fibroids from slipping into the paracolic gutters due to their weight.
After completing the morcellation of the fibroids, the surgeon proceeds to close the laparoscopic ports, marking the end of the surgical procedure.
